name: testflows-stash

metadata.name: testflows.stash
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
# [TestFlows.com Open-Source Software Testing Framework] Stash

# Why

Allows to stash values or files that are generated during test program execution
that could be reused on the next test program run.

# Usage

Use **stashed** context manager to stash a value.
If the value identified by name is found inside the stash
then the code within the **with** block is not executed.

## Example

Here is an example how to use `testflows.stash` module
and the `stashed` context manager to stash a value
returned by a function that takes a non trivial time to execute.

> example1.py
```python
import time
from testflows.stash import stashed

def generate_value():
    print("Generating a value that takes a long time...")
    time.sleep(10)
    return "my generated value"

with stashed("value") as stash:
   stash(generate_value())

print(stash.value)
```

In this example, we simulate the work done by the function using
`time.sleep(10)`. The stashed value is identified by the `name`
argument passed when creating an instance of the stash using the
`stashed()` context manager.

On the first run, when the value is not in a stash
the code within the **with** block is executed where the value
to be stashed is added by calling `stash` instance with the value
to be stashed. In this case, the result of the `generated_value()`
function.

```bash
$ python3 example1.py
Generating a value that takes a long time...
my generated value
```

Note that `stash` folder will be created in the same directory as the source file.

```bash
$ find
./stash
./stash/example1.py.stash
./example1.py
```

The `stash` folder will contain a file that stores the stashed value by using the name specified
upon creation of the stash instance. The name of the stash file will have the same prefix as the
original source file.

The content of the file will have the following

```bash
$ cat ./stash/example1.py.stash 
value = '"my generated value"'
```

On the second run, the value is found in a stash and the body of the
**with** block is skipped and the `generated_value()` is not called
and the stashed value is available using the `stash.value`
where `stash` is the instance of the `stashed()` context manager.

```bash
$ python3 example1.py
my generated value
```

## `stashed()`

The `stashed` context manager can take the following arguments.

```python
stashed(name, id=None, output=None, path="./stash", encoder=json, use_stash=True)
```

where

* `name` name of the stashed value inside the stash file
* `id` custom stash id, default: `None`
* `output` function to output the representation of the value, default: `None`
* `path` custom stash folder path, default: `./stash`
* `encoder` custom encoder for the value, default: `json`
* `use_stash` use stash, default: `True`. If `False`, then the stash will not be used
  and the **with** block is always executed regardless if the value is
  already stored in a stash

In general, stash file name format is defined as

```
<source file name>.<id>.stash
```

[TestFlows.com Open-Source Software Testing Framework]: https://testflows.com

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.pickler.encode
documentation:
Return a JSON formatted representation of value, a Python object.

```
param unpicklable

If set to False then the output will not contain the
information necessary to turn the JSON data back into Python objects,
but a simpler JSON stream is produced.

param max_depth

If set to a non-negative integer then jsonpickle will
not recurse deeper than 'max_depth' steps into the object.  Anything
deeper than 'max_depth' is represented using a Python repr() of the
object.

param make_refs

If set to False jsonpickle's referencing support is
disabled.  Objects that are id()-identical won't be preserved across
encode()/decode(), but the resulting JSON stream will be conceptually
simpler.  jsonpickle detects cyclical objects and will break the cycle
by calling repr() instead of recursing when make_refs is set False.

param keys

If set to True then jsonpickle will encode non-string
dictionary keys instead of coercing them into strings via .
This is typically what you want if you need to support Integer or
objects as dictionary keys.

param numeric_keys

Only use this option if the backend supports integer
dict keys natively.  This flag tells jsonpickle to leave numeric keys
as-is rather than conforming them to json-friendly strings.
Using keys=True is the typical solution for integer keys, so only
use this if you have a specific use case where you want to allow the
backend to handle serialization of numeric dict keys.

param warn

If set to True then jsonpickle will warn when it
returns None for an object which it cannot pickle
(e.g. file descriptors).

param max_iter

If set to a non-negative integer then jsonpickle will
consume at most  items when pickling iterators.

param use_decimal

If set to True jsonpickle will allow Decimal
instances to pass-through, with the assumption that the simplejson
backend will be used in  mode.  In order to use this mode
you will need to configure simplejson:

jsonpickle.set_encoder_options('simplejson',
                               use_decimal=True, sort_keys=True)
jsonpickle.set_decoder_options('simplejson',
                               use_decimal=True)
jsonpickle.set_preferred_backend('simplejson')

NOTE: A side-effect of the above settings is that float values will be
converted to Decimal when converting to json.

param use_base85

If possible, use base85 to encode binary data. Base85 bloats binary data
by 1/4 as opposed to base64, which expands it by 1/3. This argument is
ignored on Python 2 because it doesn't support it.

param fail_safe

If set to a function exceptions are ignored when pickling
and if a exception happens the function is called and the return value
is used as the value for the object that caused the error

param indent

When  is a non-negative integer, then JSON array
elements and object members will be pretty-printed with that indent
level.  An indent level of 0 will only insert newlines. None is
the most compact representation.  Since the default item separator is
(', ', ': '),  the output might include trailing whitespace when
indent is specified.  You can use separators=(',', ': ') to
avoid this.  This value is passed directly to the active JSON backend
library and not used by jsonpickle directly.

param separators

If separators is an (item_separator, dict_separator) tuple
then it will be used instead of the default (', ', ': ')
separators.  (',', ':') is the most compact JSON representation.
This value is passed directly to the active JSON backend library and
not used by jsonpickle directly.
```

```pycon
>>> encode('my string') == '"my string"'
True
>>> encode(36) == '36'
True
>>> encode({'foo': True}) == '{"foo": true}'
True
>>> encode({'foo': [1, 2, [3, 4]]}, max_depth=1)
'{"foo": "[1, 2, [3, 4]]"}'
```

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.pickler.Pickler.flatten
documentation:
Takes an object and returns a JSON-safe representation of it.

Simply returns any of the basic builtin datatypes

```pycon
>>> p = Pickler()
>>> p.flatten('hello world') == 'hello world'
True
>>> p.flatten(49)
49
>>> p.flatten(350.0)
350.0
>>> p.flatten(True)
True
>>> p.flatten(False)
False
>>> r = p.flatten(None)
>>> r is None
True
>>> p.flatten(False)
False
>>> p.flatten([1, 2, 3, 4])
[1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> p.flatten((1,2,))[tags.TUPLE]
[1, 2]
>>> p.flatten({'key': 'value'}) == {'key': 'value'}
True
```

modules.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle
documentation:
Python library for serializing any arbitrary object graph into JSON.

:::{warning}
jsonpickle can execute arbitrary Python code. Do not load jsonpickles from
untrusted / unauthenticated sources.
:::

jsonpickle can take almost any Python object and turn the object into JSON.
Additionally, it can reconstitute the object back into Python.

The object must be accessible globally via a module and must
inherit from object (AKA new-style classes).

Create an object:

```
class Thing(object):
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

obj = Thing('Awesome')
```

Use jsonpickle to transform the object into a JSON string:

```
import jsonpickle
frozen = jsonpickle.encode(obj)
```

Use jsonpickle to recreate a Python object from a JSON string:

```
thawed = jsonpickle.decode(frozen)
```

The new object has the same type and data, but essentially is now a copy of
the original.

```python
assert obj.name == thawed.name
```

If you will never need to load (regenerate the Python class from JSON), you can
pass in the keyword unpicklable=False to prevent extra information from being
added to JSON:

```
oneway = jsonpickle.encode(obj, unpicklable=False)
result = jsonpickle.decode(oneway)
assert obj.name == result['name'] == 'Awesome'
```
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (C) 2008 John Paulett (john -at- paulett.org)
Copyright (C) 2009, 2011, 2013 David Aguilar (davvid -at- gmail.com)
All rights reserved.
This software is licensed as described in the file COPYING, which
you should have received as part of this distribution.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.decode
documentation:
Convert a JSON string into a Python object.

The keyword argument 'keys' defaults to False.
If set to True then jsonpickle will decode non-string dictionary keys
into python objects via the jsonpickle protocol.

The keyword argument 'classes' defaults to None.
If set to a single class, or a sequence (list, set, tuple) of classes,
then the classes will be made available when constructing objects. This
can be used to give jsonpickle access to local classes that are not
available through the global module import scope.

The keyword argument 'safe' defaults to False.
If set to True, eval() is avoided, but backwards-compatible
(pre-0.7.0) deserialization of repr-serialized objects is disabled.

The keyword argument 'backend' defaults to None.
If set to an instance of jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend, jsonpickle
will use that backend for deserialization.

```pycon
>>> decode('"my string"') == 'my string'
True
>>> decode('36')
36
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler._safe_hasattr
documentation: Workaround unreliable hasattr() availability on sqlalchemy objects

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler._is_json_key
documentation: Has this key a special object that has been encoded to JSON?

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.loadclass
documentation:
Loads the module and returns the class.

```pycon
>>> cls = loadclass('datetime.datetime')
>>> cls.__name__
'datetime'
```

```pycon
>>> loadclass('does.not.exist')
```

```pycon
>>> loadclass('builtins.int')()
0
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.getargs
documentation: Return arguments suitable for __new__()

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.make_blank_classic
documentation:
Implement the mandated strategy for dealing with classic classes
which cannot be instantiated without __getinitargs__ because they
take parameters

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.loadrepr
documentation:
Returns an instance of the object from the object's repr() string.
It involves the dynamic specification of code.

```pycon
>>> obj = loadrepr('datetime/datetime.datetime.now()')
>>> obj.__class__.__name__
'datetime'
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.has_tag
documentation:
Helper class that tests to see if the obj is a dictionary
and contains a particular key/tag.

```pycon
>>> obj = {'test': 1}
>>> has_tag(obj, 'test')
True
>>> has_tag(obj, 'fail')
False
```

```pycon
>>> has_tag(42, 'fail')
False
```

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler._Proxy
documentation:
Proxies are dummy objects that are later replaced by real instances

The `restore()` function has to solve a tricky problem when pickling
objects with cyclical references -- the parent instance does not yet
exist.

The problem is that `__getnewargs__()`, `__getstate__()`, custom handlers,
and cyclical objects graphs are allowed to reference the yet-to-be-created
object via the referencing machinery.

In other words, objects are allowed to depend on themselves for
construction!

We solve this problem by placing dummy Proxy objects into the referencing
machinery so that we can construct the child objects before constructing
the parent.  Objects are initially created with Proxy attribute values
instead of real references.

We collect all objects that contain references to proxies and run
a final sweep over them to swap in the real instance.  This is done
at the very end of the top-level `restore()`.

The `instance` attribute below is replaced with the real instance
after `__new__()` has been used to construct the object and is used
when swapping proxies with real instances.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler.reset
documentation: Resets the object's internal state.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler.restore
documentation:
Restores a flattened object to its original python state.

Simply returns any of the basic builtin types

```pycon
>>> u = Unpickler()
>>> u.restore('hello world') == 'hello world'
True
>>> u.restore({'key': 'value'}) == {'key': 'value'}
True
```

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler.register_classes
documentation:
Register one or more classes

```
param classes

sequence of classes or a single class to register
```

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._swap_proxies
documentation: Replace proxies with their corresponding instances

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._restore_quopri
documentation: : For backwards compatibility with bytes data produced by older versions

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._restore_reduce
documentation:
Supports restoring with all elements of __reduce__ as per pep 307.
Assumes that iterator items (the last two) are represented as lists
as per pickler implementation.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._restore_key_fn
documentation:
Return a callable that restores keys

This function is responsible for restoring non-string keys
when we are decoding with `keys=True`.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._restore_pickled_key
documentation: Restore a possibly pickled key

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler.Unpickler._refname
documentation:
Calculates the name of the current location in the JSON stack.

This is called as jsonpickle traverses the object structure to
create references to previously-traversed objects. This allows
cyclical data structures such as doubly-linked lists.
jsonpickle ensures that duplicate python references to the same
object results in only a single JSON object definition and
special reference tags to represent each reference.

```pycon
>>> u = Unpickler()
>>> u._namestack = []
>>> u._refname() == '/'
True
>>> u._namestack = ['a']
>>> u._refname() == '/a'
True
>>> u._namestack = ['a', 'b']
>>> u._refname() == '/a/b'
True
```

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.unpickler._trivialclassic
documentation: A trivial class that can be instantiated with no args

modules.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.tags
documentation:
The jsonpickle.tags module provides the custom tags
used for pickling and unpickling Python objects.

These tags are keys into the flattened dictionaries
created by the Pickler class.  The Unpickler uses
these custom key names to identify dictionaries
that need to be specially handled.

variables.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.compat.PY3_ORDERED_DICT
documentation: Python 3.6+

modules.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util
documentation:
Helper functions for pickling and unpickling.  Most functions assist in
determining the type of an object.
Copyright (C) 2008 John Paulett (john -at- paulett.org)
Copyright (C) 2009-2018 David Aguilar (davvid -at- gmail.com)
All rights reserved.
This software is licensed as described in the file COPYING, which
you should have received as part of this distribution.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_type
documentation:
Returns True is obj is a reference to a type.

```pycon
>>> is_type(1)
False
```

```pycon
>>> is_type(object)
True
```

```pycon
>>> class Klass: pass
>>> is_type(Klass)
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_object
documentation:
Returns True is obj is a reference to an object instance.

```pycon
>>> is_object(1)
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_object(object())
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_object(lambda x: 1)
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_primitive
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a basic data type. Unicode strings,
integers, longs, floats, booleans, and None are considered primitive
and will return True when passed into *is_primitive()*

```pycon
>>> is_primitive(3)
True
>>> is_primitive([4,4])
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_enum
documentation: Is the object an enum?

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_dictionary
documentation:
Helper method for testing if the object is a dictionary.

```pycon
>>> is_dictionary({'key':'value'})
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_sequence
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a sequence (list, set, or tuple).

```pycon
>>> is_sequence([4])
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_list
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a Python list.

```pycon
>>> is_list([4])
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_set
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a Python set.

```pycon
>>> is_set(set())
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_bytes
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a bytestring.

```pycon
>>> is_bytes(b'foo')
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_unicode
documentation: Helper method to see if the object is a unicode string

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_tuple
documentation:
Helper method to see if the object is a Python tuple.

```pycon
>>> is_tuple((1,))
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_dictionary_subclass
documentation:
Returns True if *obj* is a subclass of the dict type. *obj* must be
a subclass and not the actual builtin dict.

```pycon
>>> class Temp(dict): pass
>>> is_dictionary_subclass(Temp())
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_sequence_subclass
documentation:
Returns True if *obj* is a subclass of list, set or tuple.

*obj* must be a subclass and not the actual builtin, such
as list, set, tuple, etc..

```pycon
>>> class Temp(list): pass
>>> is_sequence_subclass(Temp())
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_noncomplex
documentation:
Returns True if *obj* is a special (weird) class, that is more complex
than primitive data types, but is not a full object. Including:

> - {class}`~time.struct_time`

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_function
documentation:
Returns true if passed a function

```pycon
>>> is_function(lambda x: 1)
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_function(locals)
True
```

```pycon
>>> def method(): pass
>>> is_function(method)
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_function(1)
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_module_function
documentation:
Return True if `obj` is a module-global function

```pycon
>>> import os
>>> is_module_function(os.path.exists)
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_module_function(lambda: None)
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_module
documentation:
Returns True if passed a module

```pycon
>>> import os
>>> is_module(os)
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_picklable
documentation:
Return True if an object can be pickled

```pycon
>>> import os
>>> is_picklable('os', os)
True
```

```pycon
>>> def foo(): pass
>>> is_picklable('foo', foo)
True
```

```pycon
>>> is_picklable('foo', lambda: None)
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_installed
documentation:
Tests to see if `module` is available on the sys.path

```pycon
>>> is_installed('sys')
True
>>> is_installed('hopefullythisisnotarealmodule')
False
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.is_reducible
documentation:
Returns false if of a type which have special casing,
and should not have their __reduce__ methods used

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.in_dict
documentation:
Returns true if key exists in obj.__dict__; false if not in.
If obj.__dict__ is absent, return default

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.in_slots
documentation:
Returns true if key exists in obj.__slots__; false if not in.
If obj.__slots__ is absent, return default

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.has_reduce
documentation:
Tests if __reduce__ or __reduce_ex__ exists in the object dict or
in the class dicts of every class in the MRO *except object*.

Returns a tuple of booleans (has_reduce, has_reduce_ex)

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.translate_module_name
documentation:
Rename builtin modules to a consistent module name.

Prefer the more modern naming.

This is used so that references to Python's `builtins` module can
be loaded in both Python 2 and 3.  We remap to the "__builtin__"
name and unmap it when importing.

Map the Python2 `exceptions` module to `builtins` because
`builtins` is a superset and contains everything that is
available in `exceptions`, which makes the translation simpler.

See untranslate_module_name() for the reverse operation.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.untranslate_module_name
documentation:
Rename module names mention in JSON to names that we can import

This reverses the translation applied by translate_module_name() to
a module name available to the current version of Python.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util._0_9_6_compat_untranslate
documentation:
Provide compatibility for pickles created with jsonpickle 0.9.6 and
earlier, remapping `exceptions` and `__builtin__` to `builtins`.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.importable_name
documentation:
```pycon
>>> class Example(object):
...     pass
```

```pycon
>>> ex = Example()
>>> importable_name(ex.__class__) == 'jsonpickle.util.Example'
True
>>> importable_name(type(25)) == 'builtins.int'
True
>>> importable_name(None.__class__) == 'builtins.NoneType'
True
>>> importable_name(False.__class__) == 'builtins.bool'
True
>>> importable_name(AttributeError) == 'builtins.AttributeError'
True
```

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.b64encode
documentation: Encode binary data to ascii text in base64. Data must be bytes.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.b64decode
documentation: Decode payload - must be ascii text.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.b85encode
documentation: Encode binary data to ascii text in base85. Data must be bytes.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.b85decode
documentation: Decode payload - must be ascii text.

functions.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.util.items
documentation:
Iterate over dicts in a deterministic order

Python2 does not guarantee dict ordering, so this function
papers over the difference in behavior.  Python3 does guarantee
dict order, without use of OrderedDict, so no sorting is needed there.

modules.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers
documentation:
Custom handlers may be created to handle other objects. Each custom handler
must derive from {class}`jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler` and
implement `flatten` and `restore`.

A handler can be bound to other types by calling
{func}`jsonpickle.handlers.register`.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.Registry.get
documentation:
---
param cls_or_name: the type or its fully qualified name
param default: default value, if a matching handler is not found
---

Looks up a handler by type reference or its fully
qualified name. If a direct match
is not found, the search is performed over all
handlers registered with base=True.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.Registry.register
documentation:
Register the a custom handler for a class

```
param cls

The custom object class to handle

param handler

The custom handler class (if
None, a decorator wrapper is returned)

param base

Indicates whether the handler should
be registered for all subclasses
```

This function can be also used as a decorator
by omitting the `handler` argument:

```
@jsonpickle.handlers.register(Foo, base=True)
class FooHandler(jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler):
    pass
```

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a new handler to handle a registered type.

:Parameters:
  - `context`: reference to pickler/unpickler

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler.__call__
documentation:
This permits registering either Handler instances or classes

:Parameters:
  - `context`: reference to pickler/unpickler

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler.flatten
documentation:
Flatten `obj` into a json-friendly form and write result to `data`.

:param object obj: The object to be serialized.
:param dict data: A partially filled dictionary which will contain the
    json-friendly representation of `obj` once this method has
    finished.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler.restore
documentation:
Restore an object of the registered type from the json-friendly
representation `obj` and return it.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.BaseHandler.handles

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.ArrayHandler

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.DatetimeHandler
documentation:
Custom handler for datetime objects

Datetime objects use __reduce__, and they generate binary strings encoding
the payload. This handler encodes that payload to reconstruct the
object.

documentation: Flatten _sre.SRE_Pattern (compiled regex) objects

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.handlers.QueueHandler
documentation:
Opaquely serializes Queue objects

Queues contains mutex and condition variables which cannot be serialized.
Construct a new Queue instance when restoring.

classes.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend._verify
documentation: Ensures that we've loaded at least one JSON backend.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.enable_fallthrough
documentation:
Disable jsonpickle's fallthrough-on-error behavior

By default, jsonpickle tries the next backend when decoding or
encoding using a backend fails.

This can make it difficult to force jsonpickle to use a specific
backend, and catch errors, because the error will be suppressed and
may not be raised by the subsequent backend.

Calling `enable_backend(False)` will make jsonpickle immediately
re-raise any exceptions raised by the backends.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.load_backend
documentation:
Load a JSON backend by name.

This method loads a backend and sets up references to that
backend's loads/dumps functions and exception classes.

```
param dumps

is the name of the backend's encode method.
The method should take an object and return a string.
Defaults to 'dumps'.

param loads

names the backend's method for the reverse
operation -- returning a Python object from a string.

param loads_exc

can be either the name of the exception class
used to denote decoding errors, or it can be a direct reference
to the appropriate exception class itself.  If it is a name,
then the assumption is that an exception class of that name
can be found in the backend module's namespace.

param load

names the backend's 'load' method.

param dump

names the backend's 'dump' method.

rtype bool

True on success, False if the backend could not be loaded.
```

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.remove_backend
documentation: Remove all entries for a particular backend.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.encode
documentation:
Attempt to encode an object into JSON.

This tries the loaded backends in order and passes along the last
exception if no backend is able to encode the object.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.decode
documentation:
Attempt to decode an object from a JSON string.

This tries the loaded backends in order and passes along the last
exception if no backends are able to decode the string.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.set_preferred_backend
documentation:
Set the preferred json backend.

If a preferred backend is set then jsonpickle tries to use it
before any other backend.

For example:

```
set_preferred_backend('simplejson')
```

If the backend is not one of the built-in jsonpickle backends
(json/simplejson, or demjson) then you must load the backend
prior to calling set_preferred_backend.

AssertionError is raised if the backend has not been loaded.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.set_encoder_options
documentation:
Associate encoder-specific options with an encoder.

After calling set_encoder_options, any calls to jsonpickle's
encode method will pass the supplied args and kwargs along to
the appropriate backend's encode method.

For example:

```
set_encoder_options('simplejson', sort_keys=True, indent=4)
set_encoder_options('demjson', compactly=False)
```

See the appropriate encoder's documentation for details about
the supported arguments and keyword arguments.

methods.name: testflows.stash.contrib.jsonpickle.backend.JSONBackend.set_decoder_options
documentation:
Associate decoder-specific options with a decoder.

After calling set_decoder_options, any calls to jsonpickle's
decode method will pass the supplied args and kwargs along to
the appropriate backend's decode method.

For example:

```
set_decoder_options('simplejson', encoding='utf8', cls=JSONDecoder)
set_decoder_options('demjson', strict=True)
```

See the appropriate decoder's documentation for details about
the supported arguments and keyword arguments.

Manages encoding and decoding using various backends.

It tries these modules in this order:
    simplejson, json, demjson

simplejson is a fast and popular backend and is tried first.
json comes with Python and is tried second.
demjson is the most permissive backend and is tried last.

methods.name: testflows.stash.stash.stashed.__init__
documentation:
Stash value representation to a stored stash.

Stash files have format:

> \<test file name>.\<id>.stash

```
param name

name of the stashed value inside the stash file

param id

custom stash id, default: None

param output

function to output the representation of the value

param path

custom stash path, default: 

param encoder

custom encoder for the value, default: json

param use_stash

use stash, default: 
```
